issues_found: Source is approximately 2 years old (published 2024-04-11, TODAY is 2026-04-12) - exceeds 90-day threshold but qualifies for archival value consideration
Source was truncated in provided text - some discussion sections may be missing
market_impact field is speculative with low confidence (3) - the record itself acknowledges this is not a market-moving event
event_type 'economic_commentary' is reasonable but 'blog_post' could also apply given the informal, qualitative nature
human_review_reason: Source is historically dated (2 years old) but from a high-trust domain (federalreserve.gov). Human review needed to assess whether the qualitative, anecdotal regional intelligence from a Fed advisory council adds meaningful archival value beyond official statistical releases. The source content is accurate and well-represented, but the age and nature of the intelligence (non-FOMC, advisory council notes) warrant human judgment on retention.

corrected_fields.macro_context: The CDIAC report provides anecdotal evidence of economic conditions as of April 2024. Key macro implications include: (1) goods-side disinflation is materializing as expected, (2) services inflation remains sticky due to labor-intensive nature, (3) labor market is gradually cooling with wage growth moderating from 6-7% to 4-5%, (4) housing affordability remains a challenge despite price moderation. This qualitative evidence supports the narrative that the Fed was navigating between weakening inflation and resilient economic activity. NOTE: This is consultative advisory council input, not official FOMC deliberations or formal Fed policy output.
corrected_fields.market_structure_context: CDIAC serves as a consultative body providing regional, ground-level perspective from community depository institutions. This intelligence complements formal data releases by offering qualitative color on how financial institutions perceive economic conditions in their districts—information that may inform but does not constitute official Fed policy positions.
